headline: Ida Jane Sauke Johnson, Montana Community Figure, Born 1934
body:
The year of our Lord nineteen hundred and thirty-four saw the arrival of a new soul in Simms, Montana, a place where the Sun River Valley stretched wide and promised much, though often delivered its bounty only after a good, honest struggle. This particular soul, christened Ida Jane Sauke, was born on the fifth day of April to Alvin and Florence Sauke, and from her earliest days, she was destined to become a thread, strong and true, in the homespun fabric of her community. 

Now, a body might think that a life lived in the quiet corners of Montana, far from the clamor of the great cities, would be a simple affair. But simplicity, like a clear mountain stream, often hides depths and currents unseen from the surface. Ida Jane, after twenty-one years rooted in Simms, took a turn, as many do, towards the wider world, finding herself in San Diego, California, where she and Dale Johnson, her husband, brought three daughters into the world. But the pull of Montana, like the scent of pine after a summer rain, is a powerful thing, and after seven years, the family returned to the Sun River Valley, settling there for a dozen more. 

It was in the realm of education that Ida Jane truly cast her longest shadow. For twenty-nine years, she stood before the young, shaping minds and nurturing spirits, a task more vital than any gold strike or cattle drive. She served, too, as an Assistant County Superintendent for two and a half years, a testament to her dedication and the trust placed in her by the good folk of the county. Her own schooling began at Simms High, then led her through Montana State, the College of Great Falls, and finally to Northern Montana College, where she earned a Master's in Counseling. She taught in a string of schools—Evans, Sun River, Simms Elementary, Fort Shaw Elementary—before finding her longest tenure teaching kindergarten in Choteau for eighteen years. She had, it is said, a knack for turning out "Sugar Plums," a term of endearment for the little ones she guided through their earliest lessons. 

But her influence was not confined to the schoolhouse walls. Ida Jane was a woman of many parts, a joiner and a doer. She found camaraderie with the Doll Club ladies, the Antique Club ladies, and the Red Hat ladies, each group a testament to the varied interests that bind a community together. Her heart, however, seemed to beat strongest for history, as evidenced by her involvement with the Sun River Historical Society and her passion for leading tours of the Sun River Valley, sharing its stories with all who would listen. 

Her contributions did not go unnoticed. In 2017, the Lewis and Clark Foundation bestowed upon her their Meritorious Achievement Award, and the Portage Route Chapter followed suit in 2018 with the Bill Sherman Fellow Award. That same year, she was inducted into the Montana Cowgirl Hall of Fame, a fitting honor for a woman whose life embodied the spirit of resilience and dedication that built this state. 

Ida Jane Sauke Johnson passed from this world on January 23, 2020, leaving behind her daughters, Laurie, Kim, and Toni, seven grandchildren, ten great-grandchildren, and a host of nieces and nephews. She was preceded in death by her parents, Alvin and Florence, her sisters Bonnie, Roxanna, and Norma, and her brother Bob. Her life, though not etched in the grand pronouncements of history books, was a living testament to the power of a single individual to enrich the lives of many. As one might observe of the quiet, persistent work that builds a lasting community, it is often the steady hand and the generous heart, rather than the thunderous declaration, that truly shapes the future. Indeed, as a wise man once remarked, "The true measure of a civilization is not in its censuses, nor in the size of its cities, nor in the crops of its fields, but in the kind of men the country turns out." Ida Jane Sauke Johnson was, by that measure, a true product of Montana, and a credit to its soil.
